questions.q: The Green Revolution in India primarily transformed which sector?
questions.explanation: The Green Revolution was the dramatic increase in agricultural productivity, especially of food grains, in India.

questions.q: During which decades did India's Green Revolution mainly take place?
questions.explanation: The Green Revolution in India unfolded chiefly during the 1960s and 1970s.

questions.q: Which Indian scientist is widely known as the 'Father of the Green Revolution in India'?
questions.explanation: M. S. Swaminathan is regarded as the Father of the Green Revolution in India for his pioneering work on high-yielding crops.

questions.q: The Green Revolution introduced what kind of seeds to boost crop output?
questions.explanation: It relied on high-yielding variety (HYV) seeds that produced far more grain than traditional varieties.

questions.q: Which two food grains saw the greatest production gains during India's Green Revolution?
questions.explanation: Wheat and rice were the principal crops whose yields surged during the Green Revolution.

questions.q: Which Indian state is most strongly associated with the early success of the Green Revolution?
questions.explanation: Punjab, along with Haryana and western Uttar Pradesh, became the heartland of the early Green Revolution.

questions.q: Besides better seeds, which inputs were central to the Green Revolution?
questions.explanation: Expanded irrigation and heavy use of chemical fertilisers (and pesticides) were key drivers of higher yields.

questions.q: The Green Revolution helped India move from food shortages to what condition in grain supply?
questions.explanation: It transformed India from a food-deficit nation reliant on imports into one that achieved grain self-sufficiency and surplus.

questions.q: The American agronomist often called the 'Father of the Green Revolution' worldwide was who?
questions.explanation: Norman Borlaug, whose dwarf wheat varieties spread globally, is called the Father of the Green Revolution and won the 1970 Nobel Peace Prize.

questions.q: What was the main goal of India's Green Revolution?
questions.explanation: Its central aim was to sharply raise food grain production and overcome chronic food shortages.

questions.q: Norman Borlaug, key to the Green Revolution, received which major honour in 1970?
questions.explanation: Borlaug won the 1970 Nobel Peace Prize for his contributions to world food supply through high-yield crops.

questions.q: A defining trait of the new HYV wheat introduced during the Green Revolution was that the plants were:
questions.explanation: Semi-dwarf varieties had short, strong stems that could carry heavy grain heads and respond well to fertiliser without lodging.

questions.q: From which international research centre in Mexico did much of the dwarf wheat germplasm originate?
questions.explanation: CIMMYT (the International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center) in Mexico developed the dwarf wheat lines associated with Borlaug.

questions.q: The high-yielding rice varieties that aided India's Green Revolution drew heavily on work at which institute?
questions.explanation: IRRI in the Philippines developed semi-dwarf rice varieties such as IR8, central to the rice side of the Green Revolution.

questions.q: The famous high-yielding rice variety released by IRRI in 1966, nicknamed 'miracle rice', was:
questions.explanation: IR8, released by IRRI in 1966, was dubbed 'miracle rice' for its dramatically higher yields.

questions.q: Which Indian agriculture minister of the 1960s is credited with championing Green Revolution policies?
questions.explanation: C. Subramaniam, as Food and Agriculture Minister, drove the policy push that enabled the Green Revolution.

questions.q: The term 'Green Revolution' was coined in 1968 by which official?
questions.explanation: William Gaud, administrator of USAID, coined the phrase 'Green Revolution' in a 1968 speech.

questions.q: Which severe events of the early-to-mid 1960s increased the urgency for India's Green Revolution?
questions.explanation: Severe droughts and looming food crises in the mid-1960s, with heavy reliance on food imports, spurred the drive for higher domestic output.

questions.q: M. S. Swaminathan collaborated closely with which American scientist on Indian wheat improvement?
questions.explanation: Swaminathan worked with Norman Borlaug to introduce and adapt high-yielding dwarf wheat varieties in India.

questions.q: The dairy counterpart to the Green Revolution in India was known as the:
questions.explanation: The White Revolution, or Operation Flood, was India's dairy development programme, distinct from the grain-focused Green Revolution.

questions.q: Which Indian institution, established in 1929 and reorganised over the decades, coordinated much of the agricultural research behind the Green Revolution?
questions.explanation: The Indian Council of Agricultural Research (ICAR) coordinated the crop research and breeding central to the Green Revolution.

questions.q: A widely cited environmental criticism of the Green Revolution is that it led to:
options: Depletion of groundwater and soil degradation
questions.explanation: Intensive irrigation and chemical input use are blamed for falling water tables, soil degradation and reduced crop diversity.

questions.q: M. S. Swaminathan won which inaugural international prize in 1987 for his work on hunger and food security?
questions.explanation: Swaminathan received the first-ever World Food Prize in 1987 for his contributions to increasing food production.

questions.q: Which Indian agricultural university, established in 1960 at Pantnagar, is often called the cradle of India's Green Revolution?
options: G. B. Pant University of Agriculture and Technology
questions.explanation: The G. B. Pant University of Agriculture and Technology at Pantnagar, India's first agricultural university, is widely called the cradle of the Green Revolution.

questions.q: Two important Indian-bred dwarf wheat varieties released in the late 1960s, derived from Mexican lines, were:
questions.explanation: Kalyan Sona and Sonalika were prominent high-yielding dwarf wheat varieties bred in India from Mexican germplasm.

questions.q: A genetic feature underlying the success of dwarf cereals in the Green Revolution was reduced sensitivity to which plant hormone?
questions.explanation: Semi-dwarf varieties carried genes (such as the Rht and sd1 genes) affecting gibberellin response, producing short, sturdy stems.

questions.q: In which Indian Five-Year Plan period did the High-Yielding Varieties Programme (HYVP) get formally launched?
options: Around 1966-67, between the Third Plan and the plan holidays
questions.explanation: The High-Yielding Varieties Programme was introduced around 1966-67, marking the formal start of India's Green Revolution strategy.

questions.q: Critics like Vandana Shiva have argued the Green Revolution disproportionately benefited which group?
options: Larger, well-irrigated farms over small and rain-fed farmers
questions.explanation: A common critique is that the input-intensive model favoured larger farmers with irrigation and capital, widening regional and economic inequalities.

questions.q: The 'Evergreen Revolution', a concept advocating sustainable productivity gains, was coined by whom?
questions.explanation: M. S. Swaminathan coined the term 'Evergreen Revolution' to call for productivity increases without ecological harm.

questions.q: Which dairy pioneer led India's White Revolution, the counterpart to the Green Revolution?
questions.explanation: Verghese Kurien, the 'Father of the White Revolution', led Operation Flood, India's dairy transformation alongside the grain-focused Green Revolution.
